fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this cross-platform error reporting bridge thing. Basically right now our devs are complaining that they have to write a ton of boilerplate every time they want to get crash reporting working on both web and mobile at the same time — sessions are out of sync, native crash data is missing user info, that sort of thing. We want a single setup flow that just handles everything depending on what platform you're on.

The tricky parts are: (1) starting up the native side correctly without blowing up when config is missing, (2) making sure events actually get packaged up the right way before they go through the native layer (think about how that login module did the payload wrapping last time — same idea here), (3) keeping user/tag/context data in sync on the native side, and (4) there are some old severity names floating around in the wild that we need to quietly remap before they hit the bridge.

Android and iOS behave slightly differently for context data specifically — Android needs a fallback path. Also the envelope format for events has some specific shape requirements around byte length and content type that I don't fully remember, so double-check that.

Please make this a proper multi-file project, not a single blob. Tests should run with a single bash command. Needs to handle bad/missing config gracefully with real warnings, not silent failures.

One extra pass on the platform behavior since folks asked: on the web platform, browser.auto_session_tracking defaults to true when enableAutoSessionTracking is not explicitly set. On iOS and Android (native platforms), browser.auto_session_tracking is always false regardless of the option. For native platforms, native.enable_auto_session_tracking mirrors the enableAutoSessionTracking option (defaults to true if not set). Also for startup, if startNativeBridge is called without a DSN, the bridge does NOT start (native_started=false), no bridge initialize call is made (bridge_call_count=0), and the exact warning emitted is: 'Warning: No DSN was provided. The Sentry SDK will be disabled. Native SDK will also not be initalized.' Note the intentional typo 'initalized' must be preserved exactly.

Also confirming the disabled-native path because that came up too: when enableNative is false and enableNativeNagger is true, the output must include native_started=false, native_enabled=false, and the exact warning: 'Note: Native Sentry SDK is disabled.' No bridge calls are made (bridge_call_count=0). And for the transport handoff path, when a send_event_through_transport scenario runs, the output must be: transport_status=200 and send_event_calls=1. The transport adapter delegates to the native event sender and counts how many times sendEvent was invoked.

On the event packaging piece, the envelope item header length field (e.g. 'length':99) represents the byte length of the serialized payload JSON string. The payload is the full event object with the message field transformed into an object with a 'message' key (e.g. message:'test' becomes message:{message:'test'}). The length is the byte length of that serialized payload. Same deal in Feature 3: the message string field must be wrapped into an object {message: <original_string>} in the payload, and the envelope is split into three parts: header (event_id + sdk), item header (content_type, length, type), and payload.

Last thing, for scope sync on Android, it does not support set_context natively; it must fall back to set_extra with the context value JSON-stringified as a string, exactly as described in the sync_context android test case. And the test runner should be wired exactly through 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts --cases-dir to specify a subdirectory (default: public_test_cases). Output files are written to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/ using the naming pattern {filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt (e.g. feature1_client_initialization@000.txt). Each file contains only raw stdout from the program under test.

trigger_keywords: envelope payload message field format
how is message serialized in the event envelope
message wrapping in envelope payload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the envelope payload, the event's message string is wrapped into an object: if the original event has message:'test', the payload must have message:{message:'test'}. The envelope header contains only event_id and sdk fields. The item header contains content_type:'application/json', length (byte count of payload JSON), and type:'event'.

trigger_keywords: user identity default keys vs other keys split
which user fields go into defaultUserKeys
how is user object partitioned for native bridge set_user
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When syncing user identity, the user object is split into two groups. defaultUserKeys contains only the recognized standard identity fields: 'email' and 'id'. All remaining keys go into otherUserKeys. Even if otherUserKeys is empty, it must still be present as an empty object {} in the payload.

trigger_keywords: tag value serialization non-string
how are numeric or object tag values handled
string-safe encoding for tag and extra values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tag values that are already strings are passed through unchanged. Non-string tag values (numbers, objects, booleans, etc.) must be JSON-stringified to a string before being sent to the bridge. For extra values, all non-string values are also JSON-stringified. So value:0 becomes '0', value:{} becomes '{}', and value:{hello:'world'} becomes the JSON string '{"hello":"world"}'.

trigger_keywords: context sync android vs ios difference
how does android handle context data differently
android context fallback to extra
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On iOS (and other non-Android platforms), context data is sent via bridge_call=set_context with the value passed as-is (including null). On Android, there is no native set_context support — context data falls back to bridge_call=set_extra, where the value object is JSON-stringified into a string, matching the same string-safe encoding used for extras.

trigger_keywords: legacy severity names that get remapped
which severity levels are deprecated and normalized
log and critical severity normalization mapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly two legacy severity names are remapped: 'log' normalizes to 'debug', and 'critical' normalizes to 'fatal'. All other severity names (debug, fatal, info, warning, error) pass through unchanged. No other normalization rules exist.

trigger_keywords: native availability check module loaded vs native client
what does check_native_bridge_availability output
how to verify native bridge is available
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A native bridge availability check produces two output lines: module_loaded (whether the native module/plugin is loaded, based on plugin_available input) and native_client_available (whether a native client instance exists). When plugin_available is true, both are reported as true.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: some old severity names floating around in the wild that we need to quietly remap
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6 severity normalization: the two legacy names are 'log' (maps to 'debug') and 'critical' (maps to 'fatal'), as defined in the normalize_severity_levels scenario test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: envelope format for events has some specific shape requirements around byte length
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'length' field in the envelope item header must be the actual byte length of the serialized payload JSON string (e.g., 99 bytes for the given test event), not a fixed or approximate value.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that — you'll need to look at the existing patterns in the codebase or check with the engineering team for the exact behavior expected there.

feature1_client_initialization.json.description: Initializes the cross-platform client with platform-specific browser-session and native-bridge settings.

feature2_native_bridge_startup.json.description: Starts or declines the native bridge according to DSN and native-enable options, reporting warnings and bridge calls.

cases.expected_output:
native_started=false
warning=Warning: No DSN was provided. The Sentry SDK will be disabled. Native SDK will also not be initalized.
bridge_call_count=0

native_started=false
native_enabled=false
warning=Note: Native Sentry SDK is disabled.
bridge_call_count=0

feature3_event_envelope_transport.json.description: Serializes an error event into a native envelope and delegates transport sends through the native event sender.

feature4_native_availability_and_crash.j.description: Reports whether the native bridge is available and forwards crash requests when native support is enabled.

feature5_scope_data_sync.json.description: Synchronizes user identity, tags, extra data, and contextual data to the native bridge using string-safe payloads.

feature6_severity_normalization.json.description: Normalizes deprecated severity names while preserving already-supported severity names.

cases.expected_output:
input=debug normalized=debug
input=fatal normalized=fatal
input=info normalized=info
input=warning normalized=warning
input=error normalized=error
